mappings.rationaleAI: Scientific study of language spanning phonetics, syntax, semantics, and neurolinguistics (F, F.1, F.2). Covers the nature of language, ways of studying it through phonetics and grammar (C, C.2), speech and language processing in the brain (C.4), and linguistic change and language typology (C.7). Addresses diachronic linguistics including the comparative method and language classification (F.3, F.7). Coverage of language change across geographic space touches on dialectology and linguistic geography (F.4).

Linguistic diversity worldwide covering language classification and identification criteria (F.7, H). Examines what differentiates languages from dialects (C.3), definitions of language (C.1), and typological features including phonology, word order, and morphological systems (C). Covers the global distribution of languages including examples from ASL, Arabic, Chinese, and German.

Language in sociocultural context covering social evaluations of dialects and language variants (C.3, G.1), language and social differentiation (C.6), and language dominance and shift (G.2, G.3). Addresses language planning and policy as politically driven processes (G.4), language and gender as social group dynamics (G.5), semantic flexibility and style (C.5). Traces historical linguistics from Bopp and Grimm connecting to dialectology (F.4).

History and evolution of English from Old English through modern global varieties, covering phonological, grammatical, and lexical changes over time (C.7, F.3). English as a Germanic language within the Indo-European family (H.1.f).

Language diversity in societies covering transmission and coexistence of languages (C.6), the role of language in cross-cultural relations (G.3), language as a political instrument through nationalism and regional rights (G.4), and language in unifying social groups (G.5). Addresses endangered language decline and the connection of language with history and cultural transmission (G.2). Covers approximately 6,500-7,000 languages worldwide (H).

Slang as informal nonstandard vocabulary distinguished from dialect, jargon, and colloquialism -- a specialized language variant (C.3). Covers code-switching, register variation, and semantic flexibility (C.5). Examines slang's role in vernacular speech communities and group identity (G.5).

Translation as a fundamental mechanism of communication across languages and cultures (A), directly relevant to the role of language in cross-cultural relations (G.3). Covers how translation determines and transforms meaning in commerce, religion, literature, and political conflict.

History and development of writing systems from cuneiform and hieroglyphs to modern scripts (E.1). Covers logographic, syllabic, alphabetic systems (E.2), hieroglyphic, cuneiform, alphabetic, and ideographic scripts (E.3), and calligraphy traditions across Semitic, Arabic, Greek, Latin, and East Asian civilizations (E.5). Directly relevant to the study of writing as a linguistic subdiscipline (F.6).

Egyptian hieroglyphs as a writing system spanning three thousand years, covering the nature and evolution of this logographic-pictographic script (E.1, E.3). Addresses decipherment through the Rosetta Stone and the study of writing as a discipline (F.6).
Visual presentation of written language covering letterform design, typeface evolution, and font technology -- an adjunct to writing concerned with the visual rendering and formatting of text (E.4).

Historical and cultural survey of reading and literacy covering the evolution from oral reading to mass literacy driven by printing and public education (C.6). Connects written language to cultural transmission, language learning, and the relationship between written and spoken language (E).

History and craft of dictionaries addressing word definition, linguistic authority, and the recording of language use -- directly relevant to meaning and style in language (C.5) and semantics as the study of language and meaning (F.5).

Rhetoric as the art of persuasion foundational to civil society and democracy, a form of communication modifying human behavior (A). Covers stylistic and semantic dimensions of persuasive language (C.5) and the use of language as a political instrument across cultures (G.4).

mappings.vsiAuthor: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
mappings.rationaleAI: Philosophy of meaning examining how meaning arises in language, covering word meaning, reference, truth, formal semantics, and speech act theory (C.5, F.5). Addresses structural and lexical meanings and the relationship between language and conceptualization.
Theory and practice of interpretation examining how meaning is constructed through language, texts, and discourse (C.5, F.5). Covers the relationship between language and the mediation of knowledge.

Study of how humans process, comprehend, produce, store, and learn language -- the psychological bases of speech and language (C.4). Examines language processing mechanisms and the cognitive computations underlying communication, including the physiology of speech as brain functions (D.2).

Cognitive and neuroscientific study of dyslexia as a reading and speech disorder, covering diagnosis, brain-scanning research, and evidence-based interventions for this developmental language impairment (D.3).

Developmental psychology covering language acquisition, inner speech, and brain development underlying learning -- the psychological bases of speech development in children (C.4). Covers language learning as part of cultural transmission (C.6).
Cryptography as the science of securing information through codes, ciphers, and encryption -- directly mapping to cryptology as codes, ciphers, and other means of encrypting language (C.8).

AI applications in language processing, memory, and learning, including computational theory and machine consciousness -- relevant to cybernetic communication, computer languages, human language-computer interfaces, and artificial intelligence (B.4).
Foundational computer science covering algorithms, programming languages, and computational models -- relevant to computer languages and human language-computer interfaces (B.4).

Information as a foundational concept spanning communication, language, and the Internet (A). Covers computational foundations and information technology relevant to cybernetic communication and computer interfaces (B.4).

Poststructuralism challenging fixed meanings in language through Barthes's semiotics, Derrida's deconstruction, and Kristeva's intertextuality -- relevant to meaning and style in language, semantic flexibility (C.5), and the study of language and meaning (F.5).

Literary theory covering structuralism (Saussure), speech act theory, and identity construction through language -- relevant to meaning and style in language and conceptualization (C.5), and semantics as the study of language and meaning (F.5).
Chinese literary culture from antiquity to the present, with the Chinese language as the medium of this literary tradition within the Sino-Tibetan language family (H.6.a).
Japanese literature spanning 1,500 years, with the Japanese language as the medium of this literary tradition and cultural production (H.13).
Anthropological study of communication as the essential element in social and cultural interaction (A). Connects to linguistics through the role of language in transmitting culture (G.2).
Nonverbal communication through bodily expression, facial expression, and empathy mechanisms (B.1). Addresses cross-cultural questions about emotional expression and laughter as communication (B.2).

Covers philosophical and psychological analysis of humor including stress relief, group cohesion, and cultural universality of humor across human societies, directly relevant to laughter and nonword sounds as communication.
The relationship between thought and language, culturally specific modes of reasoning versus universal cognitive patterns, and how language relates to conceptualization (C.5).
Cross-cultural literary relations examining translation, modernity, and engaging with literature beyond one's own culture -- the role of language in cross-cultural relations (G.3).
Etruscan civilization including their undeciphered language, an important language isolate with no known linguistic relatives (H.21.b).
